title: Low-Cost Sucrose Gradient Protocol for GFP Purification from Recombinant E. coli: A Visual Fluorescence-Based Method for Resource-Limited Laboratories
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"7k0an","text":"This protocol describes a simple, cost-effective method for purifying Green Fluorescent Protein (GFP) from E. coli using a sucrose gradient. It avoids toxic chemicals and expensive chromatography systems, making it accessible and environmentally friendly. The method preserves GFP's fluorescence and is suitable for applications in biomedical imaging, molecular tagging, and gene expression education laboratories. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"9tj6v","text":"with further improvements, it could be used in research studies.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
materials_text: {"blocks":[{"key":"do49","text":"Genetically modified E. coli BL21 (DE3) pLysS with pRSET plasmid  ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4cjra","text":" Sucrose (analytical grade)  ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"7hcml","text":" Sterile distilled water  ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"cf5b1","text":" Falcon tubes (15 ml)  ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"6461n","text":" Microcentrifuge tubes (1.5 ml)  ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ekctq","text":" Ethanol (70%)  ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"clc8u","text":" Micropipettes and tips  ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"h7l4","text":" Centrifuge (R5810 and CF-10 models or equivalents)  ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"a9mto","text":" UV light source","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: Prepare four sucrose solutions: weigh 1, 2, 3, 4 g of sucrose Figure (1): Prepared sucrose weights for the experiment to be out in 10 mL sterile water in order to make 10% (Tube 1) 20% (Tube 2) 30% (Tube 3) 40% (Tube 4) Figure (2): Sucrose powder in tubes before adding distilled water Dissolve the appropriate sucrose amounts in sterile distilled water and mix until homogeneous.

Layer 2 ml of each sucrose solution sequentially into a Falcon tube using a micropipette. Begin with 40% at the bottom, followed by 30%, 20%, and finally 10% at the top. Layer gently to avoid mixing between layers. Figure (3): The gradient of sucrose layers after preparation- the layers could be visible if you spotlight a smartphone flash and move it, this can help you know if the layers interfaced. in case layers interfaces you need to redo the process.
Centrifuge 5 ml of E. coli culture at 7000 rpm, °C, for 5 minutes in a falcon tube to pellet the bacteria. Figure (4): The bacterial culture after centrifugation. the GFP is secreted to the media.

Carefully collect 1 mL of the supernatant, which contains secreted proteins, and gently layer it on top of the prepared sucrose gradient. Figure (5): The process of loading the supernatant onto the sucrosegradient Figure (6): The sucrose gradient tube after bacterial supernatantaddition

Centrifuge the gradient tube at 10000 rpm, °C, for 00:30:00 using an R5810 centrifuge. After centrifugation, observe the tube under UV light. GFP will localize between the 10- 20% sucrose layer, visible as a fluorescent band. Figure (7): Protein localization and fluorescence between 10-20% sucrosegradient concentration
Carefully aspirate the fluorescent layer using a micropipette and transfer it to a 1.5 ml microcentrifuge tube. Centrifuge this tube at 12000 rpm, °C, for 2 minutes using a CF-10 centrifuge.
Examine the pellet under UV light to confirm GFP fluorescence. Figure (8): The purified GFP exhibited higher fluorescence intensitycompared to the control water sample
Add 20 µL of 70% ethanol and 20 µL of distilled water to the GFP sample. Mix gently and centrifuge again at 12000 rpm, °C, for 4 minutes.

After centrifugation, visualize the sample under UV light. The ethanol precipitation enhances fluorescence intensity, confirming successful concentration of GFP. Figure (9): The ethanol-precipitated GFP sample demonstrated significantly higherfluorescence compared to the untreated control when examined under identical UVexposure conditions
GFP is localized between 10-20% sucrose layers. Ethanol precipitation significantly enhances fluorescence. The final product is visibly fluorescent and suitable for downstream applications.
creator: Joseph shenekji
